system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
You are presented with 5 anonymous responses (E1-E5) and 14 bipolar constructs. Rate each response on each construct on a 1-7 scale: 1 = strongly the LEFT pole, 7 = strongly the RIGHT pole, 4 = neutral or mixed.

Responses:
**E1:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option B is the right primary policy.** The decision turns on a specific asymmetry: in consumer health, trust failures are existential, capability shortfalls are merely competitive. **Why B over the alternatives:**

The local-only option (A) sounds principled but ships a structurally weaker coach. A 60-70% capability ceiling in *health guidance* isn't a minor compromise — it directly affects whether the product gives users useful or mediocre advice on conditions...

**E2:** Given the sensitive nature of health data and the high expectations of a 2M-user consumer app, **Option B — encrypted cloud with no training** — is the superior choice. It uniquely delivers full AI capability without compromising privacy, sidestepping the regulatory quagmire and trust erosion that plague data-hungry alternatives. Health coaching demands the best possible model; a local-only approach (Option A) sacrifices too much quality at a critical moment when users are judging the feature’s utility.

**E3:** I strongly recommend **Option B: Encrypted cloud with no training**. For a direct-to-consumer health app with two million active users, trust is not just a brand pillar; it is the entire foundation of the enterprise. When dealing with highly sensitive personal health data, the margin for error regarding data privacy is zero.

**E4:** I recommend **Option B: encrypted cloud processing with no training on user health data** as the primary policy. For a direct-to-consumer health app, the core asset is not only model quality; it is user trust. Health data is among the most sensitive categories of personal information, and an AI coaching feature will likely handle behavioral, biometric, medication, reproductive, mental health, and chronic-condition signals.

**E5:** **Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted cloud with no training.**

For a consumer health app, trust is the product. Users do not compartmentalize “health data” from “identity”; a breach or even the perception of data exploitation destroys adoption faster than any feature attracts it. Option B is the only architecture that delivers full AI capability without creating a retained data footprint that must be defended forever.

Constructs (id : left pole ↔ right pole):
  C_M1_1: 'Grounds recommendation in specific health data categories (E4, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Argues from general capability-versus-privacy tradeoff (E2)'
  C_M1_2: 'Frames trust as foundational/existential to product (E3, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames choice as regulatory and quality optimization (E2)'
  C_M1_3: 'Uses vivid, absolutist rhetoric about trust (E3, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Enumerates concrete sensitive data types pragmatically (E4)'
  C_M2_1: 'Strategic trust-and-capability rhetoric'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Concrete health-data risk inventory'
  C_M2_2: 'Trust-first privacy absolutism'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Explicit alternative-by-alternative trade-off analysis'
  C_M2_3: 'Explicit local-only capability critique'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Pure privacy-and-trust rationale'
  C_M3_1: 'Focuses purely on data sensitivity and user trust'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Balances privacy needs with AI model capability constraints'
  C_M3_2: 'Rejects local-only alternatives due to inferior AI performance'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames choice entirely around avoiding retained data footprints'
  C_M4_1: 'Critiques local option's AI quality'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Omits local option critique'
  C_M4_2: 'Omits regulatory concerns'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Mentions regulatory avoidance'
  C_M4_3: 'Acknowledges model capability'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Focuses exclusively on trust'
  C_M5_1: 'Evaluates capability limitations of local processing'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Focuses solely on data privacy breach risks'
  C_M5_2: 'Acknowledges model quality as a competing priority'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Treats trust as the exclusive imperative'
  C_M5_3: 'Provides specific technical or categorical details'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Relies on abstract foundational principles'

OUTPUT FORMAT (strict). Write exactly 70 lines, no commentary, no markdown fences, no headers. One line per (construct, element) pair, in this exact format:
<construct_id>,<element>,<rating>

Example (showing the format only - your numbers will differ):
C_M1_1,E1,1
C_M1_1,E2,2
C_M1_1,E3,3
C_M1_2,E1,4
...

Constraints:
- rating is an integer from 1 to 7
- iterate all 14 constructs in the order listed, and within each construct all 5 elements in order (E1-E5)
- produce 70 lines total
- output ONLY the lines, nothing before or after
